metadata.title: Global trends in green consumption: analyzing market acceptance and sentiment through Instagram data mining

abstract:

Decisions to be made regarding the solution of the problem cause interaction between contradictory issues and the motivational and practical complexity of green consumption (Doung, 2024). The most effective way to solve this complexity is to appeal to consumers' sentiment. Sentiment, which is an important part of the value perceived by the consumer, reflects consumers' reactions to the product and service (Li et al., 2023). But, locally reached consumer sentiments may not provide a clear idea of the green consumption complex. A clearer and more understandable understanding of this phenomenon can only be possible by looking at it on a global basis. Revealing the sentiments of consumers on a global basis can be achieved through social media (ElHaffar et al., 2020).

Social media is a platform where all kinds of information can be shared and accessed globally (Lakatos et al., 2021). Therefore, it is an effective platform to manage consumers' green consumption trends. Among the social media channels, Instagram, which gained popularity with the emergence of the smartphone and the spread of the internet, has become a social networking tool preferred by all kinds of people (Latiff & Safiee, 2015a, b). Instagram will provide easy access to various consumer sentiment regarding green consumption.

sections.paper_id: Balcıoğlu et al. - 2025 - Global trends in green consumption analyzing market acceptance and sentiment through Instagram data
paragraphs: At the end of the 20th century, environmental reforms focused primarily on the production sector (Doung, 2024). But today, they are trying to draw attention to consumption and consumers because the basis of environmental awareness is based on individual awareness (Chen et al., 2019). In order to encourage and increase the consumption of green products, it is necessary to focus on consumers' decision-making processes regarding what they consume (Paul et al., 2016a, b). Consumers may have problems in the decision-making process But it is understood that the issue of revealing green consumption in a global sense through consumer sentiment has been largely neglected in previous literature (Doung, 2024).

The purpose of this research is to explore global consumer sentiment toward green products using Instagram data mining, focusing on understanding how various attributes such as eco-friendliness, price, functionality, and quality influence consumer attitudes across different product categories. This study uniquely contributes by:

• Analyzing a large dataset of Instagram comments (109,417) to uncover consumer preferences and concerns about green products. • Employing advanced analytical methods-including sentiment analysis, word frequency, and Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topic modeling-to identify and compare key attributes influencing positive and negative consumer sentiments. • Providing practical insights for businesses and policymakers to align product strategies with global consumer values and preferences, thereby advancing sustainable consumption.
• By integrating consumer sentiment analysis with social media data, this research aims to offer actionable knowledge for fostering a more sustainable global market for green products.

This study is particularly relevant because it emphasizes the significance of taking a balanced approach to product design and marketing, taking into account both environmental sustainability and consumer value. These contributions highlight the necessity for targeted initiatives that correspond with consumer values and preferences as revealed by social media analytics, resulting in a more sustainable global market for green products (Dong & Hauschild, 2017). This research is grounded in the context of global consumer sentiment toward green products, using Instagram as a platform that reflects diverse cultural and regional perspectives. The choice of Instagram as the data source is significant for the following reasons:

• Instagram is a widely used platform across various demographic and cultural groups, providing insights into consumer attitudes from different regions, socio-economic backgrounds, and age groups. This diversity allows for a comprehensive understanding of global green consumption trends (Latiff & Safiee, 2015a, b). • Research suggests that cultural and regional contexts significantly influence green consumption. For example, consumers in developed nations often prioritize ethical and environmental considerations, whereas affordability and accessibility are more prominent concerns in developing nations (Mamun et al., 2018;Yadav & Pathak, 2016). This study captures these cultural nuances by analyzing a large-scale dataset encompassing diverse global perspectives. • Social media platforms like Instagram enable consumers to voice their preferences, concerns, and sentiments in real time, reflecting cultural attitudes toward sustainability. This is particularly relevant in the context of rising environmental awareness and the global trend toward sustainable consumption (Nguyen et al., 2018). • By analyzing user-generated content on Instagram, the study provides a snapshot of evolving consumer attitudes, shaped by cultural trends, market practices, and environmental movements. This approach accounts for the dynamic nature of green consumption and its interaction with cultural values and societal expectations.

Through this culturally informed lens, the research explores how global consumer sentiment toward green products varies based on diverse contextual factors, offering insights that are both regionally nuanced and globally relevant. This study uses real green consumption data on the sentiment of Instagram users to show real results. This provides reliable practical evidence and references for consumers on green consumption. Therefore, the aim of this article is to reveal global trends in green consumption by analyzing market acceptance and sentiment through Instagram data mining.

sections.header_raw: The concept and connotation of sustainability and green phenomenon
sections.header_norm: the concept and connotation of sustainability and green phenomenon
paragraphs: The concept of sustainability emerged to solve environmental problems. In this context, sustainability is considered a multifaceted concept that covers many issues such as reducing the energy footprint, reusing and recycling (Mankoff et al., 2007;Li et al., 2023). In other words, this phenomenon can be defined as the most effective and efficient way to meet today's needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs (Dong & Hauschild, 2017). This concept aims to:

1. It introduces sustainability as a balance between economic, environmental, and social dimensions, emphasizing its role in creating a livable future. 2. It discusses the necessity of reducing environmental degradation and preserving resources for future generations as integral to sustainable development.

3. It links the concept of sustainability to practical applications such as green products, green innovation, green supply chain management, and green chemistry. These are presented as tools for mitigating environmental challenges and promoting sustainable consumption patterns. 4. By discussing the interplay between demand, supply, and environmental impact, the section frames green consumption as a critical solution to achieving sustainability.

Sustainability is the paradigm that builds a future where economic, environmental and social dimensions are intertwined and balanced in the pursuit of a better quality of life (Ralph & Stubbs, 2014). The concept can be defined as humanity leaving a livable world for future generations.

In this context, humanity is expected to act by being aware of the variables and behaviors that affect the complex balance of the world's social, ecological and economic systems (Ferdig, 2007;Paul et al., 2016a, b). Sustainability has become important criteria to develop any products (Rangappa et al., 2020). Sustainable development seems possible by carrying out the three pillars of sustainability in a balanced manner. Therefore, for sustainable development, a balance must be found between economic development, environmental impact and social equality (Sheldon, 2017). Sheldon (2017) reveals this triple balance with a scheme as follows:

In the Fig. 1 put forward by Sheldon (2017), it is understood that the three basic pillars are intertwined. But, understanding sustainability is possible by making a clear distinction between development and environmental protection (Goodland, 1995). Because it is a fact that the environment is damaged for the sake of economic development. On the other hand, it is not possible to talk about sustainable development without ensuring environmental sustainability. Because environmental resources are needed for sustainable development. When sustainable development is viewed from an economic perspective, ensuring environmental sustainability can prevent the environmental negativities that economic activities may cause from being transferred to future generations (Foy, 1990). For sustainable development, it is expected that the economic and environmental pillars as well as the social pillars are in harmony. Morelli (2011) points out that society must comply with a number of principles such as equality, freedom, and acceptance for sustainable development.

Achieving economy-based sustainability has harmful consequences on environmental sustainability. Climate change, acidification, ozone layer depletion, chemical pollution, fresh water use, biosphere integrity and change in biodiversity are the main harmful consequences of economic sustainability on environmental sustainability (Dong & Hauschild, 2017). In order to protect the environment, it should be adopted a preventive approach to environmental problems (Chen, 2007). To find a solution for the environmental problems, concepts that draw attention to green phenomenon, such as green product, green chemistry, green innovation, green supply chain management and green consumption have emerged.

The term of green product can be defined as the products that will not pollute the earth, in other words; natural management. The concept of green indicates that the term specifically focuses on environmental sustainability. So, the term is defined as integrating environmental concerns into the inter-organizational practices (Sarkis et al., 2011). The importance of green supply chain management arises from the destruction of the environment, the decrease in raw material resources, the overflow of waste sites and increasing pollution levels (Srivastava, 2007). Green supply chain management increases environmental performance among companies and their partners by minimizing waste and reducing cost to increase corporate image, competitive advantage and marketing visibility (Rao & Holt, 2005).

The concept of green chemistry was first highlighted in 1987 in a report belonging to The World Commission on Environmental Development. The term is defined as the design of chemical products and processes to reduce or eliminate the use and generation of hazardous substances (Anastas & Eghbali, 2009). Green chemistry contributes to the sustainability cycle by minimizing environmental and health hazards (Horvath & Anastas, 2007). From this point, the concepts of green chemistry and sustainability can be seen as intertwined phenomena (Sheldon, 2012). Green chemistry, which can meet environmental and economic targets simultaneously to ensure environmental sustainability, can be defined as a field where studies are carried out on chemical basis (Anastas & Eghbali, 2009).

In fact, every concept related to the green phenomenon is a matter of supply and demand. Supply cannot be created without demand. Supply and demand are particularly related to how much consumers understand the green concept and how much they need this concept. Consumers' increasing perception of the green concept and their consumption of green products draw attention to the concept of green consumption.

paragraphs: Excessive consumption, which has become a major problem in developed and industrialized countries, can cause environmental corruption. To prevent this corruption, it is necessary to switch to more sustainable consumption patterns (Tanner & Kast, 2003). Green consumption lies at the core of sustainable consumption. A sustainable consumption habit can be provided by supporting and encouraging the consumption of green products (Paul et al., 2016a, b). Recent studies highlight the significant role of social influence in promoting green consumption, demonstrating that exposure to coworkers' green behaviors in the workplace can positively impact an observer's green purchasing decisions (Yao, 2024). This effect is mediated by environmental concern and further moderated by the perceived status of resources can be recycled or conserved (Gonçalves et al., 2016;Paul et al., 2016a, b). The purpose of using green product is to prevent, reduce and fix the environmental damage that may occur. In this context, green product is a way to solve environmental problems, and produce products and services that are beneficial to the environment (Lin & Huang, 2012a, b). Due to the effects of catastrophic environmental pollution and global warming, humanity has started to turn to green products in order to protect the environment (Chen & Chang, 2013). While manufacturers produce green products through green marketing, consumers are directed to use and consume green products responsibly by prioritizing their environmental responsibilities (Hong & Guo, 2019). It can be said that the main purpose of fronting to green products is to protect our damaged water, soil and air, in other words, our environment that we will inherit to the future.

Green innovation can be defined as energy saving, finding clear energy, pollution prevention, waste recycling, green product designs and development of products (Berrone et al., 2013;Chen et al., 2006). In this context, green innovation is considered as the fundamental factor to ensure the expansion of the green environment, its sustainability and a better quality of life (Dangelico & Pujari, 2010). In order to achieve this, it is necessary to eliminate any kind of environmental pollution by reducing it. But environmental pollution caused by the inefficient use of natural resources places a great financial burden on manufacturers. In particular, businesses appear to be trying to increase resource efficiency through green innovation to reduce this cost (Chen et al., 2006). In the international arena, companies have focused on the production and supply of products that do not pose environmental hazards in line with the demands of their customers (Chiou et al., 2011). It can be said that the companies' aim in this trend is to clear their negative environmental image that they have. And the increase in environmental awareness of customers causes companies to move in this direction.

Green supply chain management refers to the responsibility of industry and businesses to ensure coordination for environmental, social and ethical compliance throughout their supply chains, as well as participation in the green purchasing process, which is important for big and multinational companies (Tseng et al., 2013). The term has gained popularity in the Southeast Asia region, is a way for organizations in this region to demonstrate their belief and commitment to sustainability (Rao & Holt, 2005). The concepts of green and management included in the term draw attention to the three basic pillars of sustainability. Fahimnia et al. (2015) states that the successful integration of economic, environmental and social sustainability goals has been at the forethought of leading supply chain and operations

(2015) states that the successful integration of economic, environmental and social sustainability goals has been at the forethought of leading supply chain and operations

Excessive consumption, which has become a major problem in developed and industrialized countries, can cause environmental corruption. To prevent this corruption, it is necessary to switch to more sustainable consumption patterns (Tanner & Kast, 2003). Green consumption lies at the core of sustainable consumption. A sustainable consumption habit can be provided by supporting and encouraging the consumption of green products (Paul et al., 2016a, b). Recent studies highlight the significant role of social influence in promoting green consumption, demonstrating that exposure to coworkers' green behaviors in the workplace can positively impact an observer's green purchasing decisions (Yao, 2024). This effect is mediated by environmental concern and further moderated by the perceived status of resources can be recycled or conserved (Gonçalves et al., 2016;Paul et al., 2016a, b). The purpose of using green product is to prevent, reduce and fix the environmental damage that may occur. In this context, green product is a way to solve environmental problems, and produce products and services that are beneficial to the environment (Lin & Huang, 2012a, b). Due to the effects of catastrophic environmental pollution and global warming, humanity has started to turn to green products in order to protect the environment (Chen & Chang, 2013). While manufacturers produce green products through green marketing, consumers are directed to use and consume green products responsibly by prioritizing their environmental responsibilities (Hong & Guo, 2019). It can be said that the main purpose of fronting to green products is to protect our damaged water, soil and air, in other words, our environment that we will inherit to the future.

sections.paper_id: Balcıoğlu et al. - 2025 - Global trends in green consumption analyzing market acceptance and sentiment through Instagram data
paragraphs: Green consumption values are collected as functional, social, emotional, conditional and epistemic under five main headings and described them as follows (Goçalves et al., 2016;Biswas and Roy, 2015;Lin & Huang, 2012a, b):

• Functional value refers to the alternative capacity for functional, utilitarian or physical performance such as perceived usefulness, reliability, durability and price for consumers. • Social value is the perceived benefit obtained as a result of communication in different social groups. • Emotional value is the perceived benefit derived from the capacity to evoke emotions or emotional states. • Conditional value is the perceived benefit of obtaining a result of a particular situation or condition facing the decision maker. • Epistemic value is the effect of information and satisfaction on all stages of the decision-making process in green consumption. Lin and Huang (2012a, b) point out that all values have a positive effect on green consumers. In addition to having these values, green consumers are expected to have sufficient environmental knowledge and perception. Especially, environmental knowledge of consumers plays a significant role of green consumption. Environmental knowledge represents what consumers know about fundamental relationships with the environment that have significant ecological impacts (Pagiaslis & Krontalis, 2014). It may be said that the consumers with a high level of environmental knowledge are more likely to consume green products and have higher environmental concerns. And even companies renew and develop their environmental knowledge in order to meet green consumption demands.

Companies around the world are developing common green marketing strategies to meet the increasing environmental concerns of consumers (Nguyen et al., 2018). As the world is changing and it is under the effect of neoliberalism. Although environmental factors are at the forefront, neoliberalism can still use this positive trend for its own benefit in the background. Whether under the influence of neoliberalism or not, the phenomenon of green consumption has now taken its place among the premises of the world community for sustainable development. Green markets are being expanded for a more environmental, efficient and sustainable development. The main goal here is to expand the green consumption network by increasing consumers' marketing acceptance.

The current article focuses on global trends in green consumption. However, we believe that familiarizing the reader with key research into green consumption might better prepare them for the discussion of analyzing market the coworker, emphasizing the importance of workplace dynamics in fostering sustainable practices.

The concept of green consumption first emerged as the focus and research topic of policy makers and marketing strategies (Peattie, 2010). But green consumption is a problematic concept. There is a theoretical and semantic conflict between green and consumption. While the concept of green evokes the protection of environmental resources, the concept of consumption means their damage and destruction (Evans, 2011). It may be thought that the concept of green refers only to environmental issues, but this concept is intertwined with the social and economic elements of sustainable development (Yang et al., 2015;Peattie, 2010). For example, although purchasing organic products is thought to be a behavior related to protecting the environment, consumers also perceive it as taste and personal health benefits (Peattie, 2010). The main reason why consumers prefer green products include the environmental friendliness of these products, their sensitivity towards the environment and the feeling of doing good (Lin & Huang, 2012a, b). But, it cannot be said that all consumers are willing to consume green products and all producers are willing to produce green products. The main factor of this issue the high product cost and accessibility.

The consumers who prefer consume green product (choosing to purchase environmentally friendly products) are called as green consumers (Cronin et al., 2011). These consumers adopt sustainable lifestyles and consumption of sustainable products and services that not only do not harm the environment but also do not compromise on the future (Gonçalves et al., 2016). Green consumers, whose numbers are increasing day by day, attract the attention of manufacturers. In order to meet the needs of consumers, manufacturers have to switch to environmentally friendly production that does not harm the environment. This way is one of the solutions to environmental problems. In general, it may be better to focus on the individual in solving environmental problems.

The solution to environmental problems that threaten human life depends on the development of leaner and more efficient technologies, as well as urgent and significant changes in human behavior to balance consumption (Tanner & Kast, 2003). The development of environmentally sustainable balanced consumption and production systems depends on consumers' display of green consumption behaviors and their willingness to participate in these behaviors (Peattie, 2010). Although governments and green groups support green consumption, consumer decisions can be influenced by consumption values (Lin & Huang, 2012a, b). The green consumption values are defined as the tendency to express the value of environmental protection through one's purchases and consumption behaviors (Haws et al., 2014). 2021). However, obtaining and consuming these products requires a certain budget and spending habit. In general, the production and marketing of these products is more expensive than mass products. Bringing green supply to green demand is achieved through green marketing. Understanding the characteristics of green products, identifying and understanding consumer payment habits, and managing sales channels and promotional tools will be very useful for companies aiming to design, develop and market green products (Jalali & Khalid, 2019).

Consumer trust is an essential prerequisite for creating a market for reliable products, such as green products, even if they are highly priced (Sailer et al., 2022). Companies gain consumer trust by clearly demonstrating the benefits of green marketing to people and the planet (Cronin et al., 2011). For the sustainability of green marketing, simply gaining consumer trust may not be enough. To promote green product consumption, marketers must focus on consumer preferences and decision-making processes (Paul et al., 2016a, b). This process is generally related to the consumer's spending habits and priorities. In other words, a choice regarding green product consumption depends primarily on your spending cycle. Young et al. (2010) presented the spending process of consumers on green products with a diagram in stages. Young et al. (2010) tries to explain the spending process of consumers on green products with a model in stages (Fig. 2).

From the green consumer purchasing model, it is understood that there is a purchasing circulation and steps in this circulation follows each other. The steps start with a general awareness and it reaches an end with a specific awareness related to green consumption. Briefly, Young et al. (2010) explains the model as follows:

• The green values possessed by the consumer enable the formation of green criteria regarding consumption in the next stage. • Choosing green criteria for purchasing is related to compliance with green ethics, and the consumer can obtain information from social media and the environment. • Green criteria may create obstacles to consumer attitudes towards green purchasing, and these obstacles can be overcome with awareness. • Although there are obstacles that hinder the consumer's purchasing decision, those that make purchasing easier pave the way for green consumption. • The purchasing process made by a green consumer is different each time, and the experience and knowledge gained from each purchasing process affects the previous green purchasing process.

Therefore, we present Table 1, where key information regarding scholarly study focusing on green consumption is presented. Given the magnitude of research focused on green consumption in many different sectors. Additionally, we have tried to include those articles that are relatively more cited.

A quick overview of the key findings of the papers above indicates that previous research has focused on various aspects of green consumption including its drivers, impacts, and implications. When the findings are examined, it can be seen that a common pattern regarding drivers or implications of green consumption does not exist. And it is also seemed that there are qualified studies on green consumption, but it hasn't been found any study that reveals consumers' perceptions regarding green consumption clearly and deeply by analyzing market acceptance and sentiment through Instagram data mining. As the social media platform offers a huge source of data from all segments of society.

Recent studies have emphasized the increasing importance of consumer preferences and behavior in promoting sustainable consumption. For instance, research highlights that consumers often prioritize environmental impact, quality, and health benefits when choosing green products, though financial constraints and accessibility remain significant barriers (Yadav & Pathak, 2016;Lin & Huang, 2012a, b). The role of social and emotional values, such as ethical sourcing and aesthetic appeal, has also gained prominence in driving green consumption behavior (Gonçalves et al., 2016;Biswas & Roy, 2015). However, prior studies have largely focused on survey-based approaches or regional analyses, leaving a gap in understanding global consumer sentiment and preferences on a social media platform. This study addresses these gaps by leveraging Instagram data to analyze worldwide consumer attitudes, offering a unique lens to examine the intersection of eco-friendliness, pricing, quality, and functionality across diverse green product categories.

green consumption (Gonçalves et al., 2016). Additionally, barriers such as financial constraints and limited accessibility are consistently noted as critical challenges to green product adoption (Paul et al., 2016a, b;Biswas & Roy, 2015). Perceptions of greenwashing and skepticism regarding sustainability claims further hinder consumer trust and adoption (Cronin et al., 2011). sections.header_raw: Summary of research designs in relevant studies

paragraphs: Research on green consumption has employed a variety of research designs to explore consumer attitudes and behaviors:

• Many studies, such as those by Yadav and Pathak (2016) and Gonçalves et al. (2016), rely on surveys to gather consumer insights regarding green products. These studies use structured questionnaires to quantify attitudes, preferences, and willingness to pay for sustainable products. • Experimental designs, as employed by Biswas and Roy (2015), test consumer responses to specific green product attributes under controlled conditions. These experiments often examine trade-offs between price, quality, and environmental features. • Focus groups and interviews, as seen in studies by Lin and Huang (2012a, b), provide detailed, subjective insights into consumer motivations and perceptions. • Recent studies, such as Latiff and Safiee (2015a, b), utilize data from social platforms to analyze trends and consumer sentiment in real time, offering a broad, dynamic view of green consumption.

This study addresses several critical research gaps identified in the existing literature on green consumption, which are directly relevant to the research questions: Gap 1: Limited Understanding of How Multiple Product Attributes Shape Consumer Sentiment.

• While prior research explores the role of individual attributes, such as eco-friendliness or pricing, studies rarely examine how multiple attributes (e.g., functionality, quality, and price) interact to influence consumer sentiment toward green products (Paul et al., 2016a, b;Yadav & Pathak, 2016).
Gap 2: Insufficient Analysis of Category-Specific Variations in Green Consumption.

• Most studies provide a generalized view of green consumption, neglecting the nuances of consumer preferences and behaviors across specific product categories such as sustainable fashion, renewable energy solutions, and zero-waste products (Dong & Hauschild, 2017;Nguyen et al., 2018).
Gap 3: Overlooking the Role of Non-Green Attributes in Shaping Consumer Evaluations.

• While green attributes like sustainability and ecofriendliness have been well-studied, the influence of non-green attributes, such as functionality, pricing, and brand perception, remains underexplored in consumer assessments of green products (Gonçalves et al., 2016;Biswas & Roy, 2015).
Gap 4: Lack of Large-Scale, Real-Time Analysis of Consumer Sentiment.

• Traditional methods, such as surveys and experiments, often capture a static or localized snapshot of consumer behavior, which may not reflect dynamic global trends social media analysis leverages large, global datasets that reflect real-world consumer interactions. • Qualitative methods provide in-depth insights but are less scalable, while sentiment analysis on social media offers a macro-level understanding but lacks the granularity of direct consumer feedback. • Social media analysis excels in capturing current trends and evolving attitudes, whereas traditional survey and experimental methods are often slower and retrospective.

paragraphs: How do these qualities impact green consumption across different product categories?

• Associated Research Gap: Limited studies explore category-specific variations in consumer preferences for Economic Forum's Insight Report on Circular Economy" and "Global Green Energy Report" by the International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA). These reports highlight the significance of several green consumption areas, reflecting current global trends and consumer interests in sustainability. Based on these insights, we select categories that are prevalent in discussions around green consumption on social media platforms, particularly Instagram, where users frequently share experiences, reviews, and recommendations. The selected categories are: 1. Including eco-friendly clothing, accessories made from sustainable materials, and brands that follow ethical manufacturing processes. 2. Products designed to minimize waste in daily life, such as reusable containers, compostable items, and products with minimal to no packaging. 3. Solar-powered gadgets, eco-friendly home energy solutions, and other green technologies aimed at reducing carbon footprints. 4. Organic produce, plant-based products, sustainable farming, and ethically sourced foods.

For the selection of specific products within these categories, the study refers to globally recognized certifications and standards, such as the Global Organic Textile Standard (GOTS) for sustainable fashion, and the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) standards for environmental management. The study focuses on products that have high visibility and engagement on Instagram, leveraging hashtags and trends analysis to identify them.

Considering the diversity and availability of data on Instagram, we identify specific products that are frequently mentioned and tagged, reflecting a wide range of consumer sentiments and discussions. The selected products for this study are (Table 2): 1. Sustainable Fashion: Eco-friendly sneakers and recycled fabric apparel. 2. Zero Waste Products: Reusable water bottles and biodegradable cleaning sponges. 3. Renewable Energy Solutions: Portable solar chargers and energy-efficient LED lighting. 4. Sustainable Food and Beverages: Organic coffee and plant-based meat alternatives.

sections.paper_id: Balcıoğlu et al. - 2025 - Global trends in green consumption analyzing market acceptance and sentiment through Instagram data
paragraphs: Instagram data, comprising captions, comments, and hashtags, often includes informal language, emojis, and various languages, reflecting the platform's diverse and global user base. Text preprocessing is thus a crucial step to ensure the data is in a suitable format for analysis. The preprocessing steps include:

• Cleaning: Removing URLs, hashtags, mentions, and non-alphabetic characters to focus on the textual content. • Normalization: Converting text to lowercase to ensure consistency across the dataset. • Tokenization: Splitting text into individual words or tokens to facilitate further analysis. • Stop Word Removal: Eliminating common words (e.g., "the," "and," "is") that do not contribute to sentiment analysis. • Stemming/Lemmatization: Reducing words to their base or root form to standardize variations of the same word.

green consumption. Instagram's extensive user base and diverse content make it an ideal source for mining data on consumer sentiment towards green products across different categories. The study utilized a comprehensive dataset comprising 109,417 comments from Instagram posts. These comments were specifically selected for their relevance to green products, representing a wide array of consumer sentiments, opinions, and discussions surrounding sustainability and eco-friendly consumption. Python, a versatile programming language, served as the primary tool for data collection, preprocessing, and analysis. Python libraries such as pandas for data manipulation, NumPy for numerical analysis, and matplotlib and seaborn for data visualization were extensively used. For sentiment analysis and LDA topic modeling, libraries such as nltk (Natural Language Toolkit), TextBlob, and gensim were instrumental. Additionally, data scraping tools compatible with Instagram's API facilitated the efficient extraction of comments.

Reasons for Choosing Instagram 1. Instagram has a vast, globally dispersed user base, making it a valuable source for capturing a wide variety of perspectives on green consumption. 2. The platform's focus on visual content, including images and videos, provides deeper insights into consumer behaviors and preferences regarding green products. 3. Instagram's features, such as hashtags, comments, and stories, facilitate high levels of user engagement and interaction, offering a dynamic view of consumer sentiments.

paragraphs: The objective is to discern the focal points of discussions around green consumption on Instagram. This involves identifying the attributes of green products and sustainability practices that users emphasize in their posts and comments. The methodology for word frequency analysis involves several tailored steps to suit the Instagram data:

Data Preprocessing: After the initial text preprocessing, further refinement is done to exclude common but irrelevant words specific to Instagram (e.g., "like4like", "instagood") and focus on terms directly related to green consumption. Frequency Count: Utilizing NLP tools to count the frequency of remaining words, giving priority to those directly related to green consumption attributes such as "sustainable," "eco-friendly," "organic," "zero waste," and "recyclable." Attribute Identification: Mapping high-frequency words to specific green consumption attributes, such as product sustainability, ethical manufacturing, waste reduction, and environmental impact. Visualization: Generating word frequency charts for the most mentioned attributes across different green product categories identified in the study (as outlined in Table 1).

This table encapsulates the findings from the word frequency analysis, presenting a clear and organized overview of the key terms associated with each green product category and specific product as discussed on Instagram. It highlights the consumer focus on various attributes of green products, ranging from sustainability and ethical manufacturing to efficiency and health benefits.

sections.paper_id: Balcıoğlu et al. - 2025 - Global trends in green consumption analyzing market acceptance and sentiment through Instagram data
paragraphs: Sentiment analysis on Instagram data involves understanding the emotions and opinions expressed in captions and comments regarding green consumption. This process, also known as opinion mining, applies natural language processing (NLP) and computational techniques to identify, extract, and quantify sentiment polarities within the data. For this study, we employ the following approaches:

• Machine Learning Models: Utilizing pre-trained models such as VADER (Valence Aware Dictionary and sEntiment Reasoner) or TextBlob, specifically designed to handle social media texts. These tools are adept at interpreting the nuances of language used on platforms like Instagram, including slang and emojis. • Custom NLP Pipeline: Building a custom sentiment analysis pipeline that incorporates machine learning algorithms (e.g., Naive Bayes, SVM) trained on a labeled dataset of Instagram posts and comments. This approach allows for fine-tuning the model to better recognize sentiments related to green consumption. • Emotion Analysis: Extending beyond positive and negative sentiment classification to identify specific emotions (e.g., joy, trust, fear) that may provide deeper insights into consumer attitudes towards green products.

The sentiment analysis will quantify the sentiment scores, categorizing them into positive, negative, and neutral sentiments. This quantification facilitates an aggregate analysis of global sentiments towards green consumption, enabling the identification of trends, patterns, and shifts in consumer attitudes over time.

Through text preprocessing and sentiment analysis, this study aims to uncover the global sentiment landscape of green consumption on Instagram, offering insights into "conveient." It reflects the community's enthusiasm for products that contribute to a circular economy, emphasizing solutions that prevent waste from reaching landfills.

The size of each term underscores the community's focus on reducing waste, embracing recycling, and supporting the use of sustainable materials in the fashion industry.

paragraphs: After sentiment analysis categorizes Instagram content into positive and negative sentiments, the Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topic model is applied to both sets of data. This unsupervised learning model helps discover the main Table 3 shows a clear and insightful overview of consumer satisfaction rates across different categories of green products based on sentiment analysis of Instagram content. The table reveals that Sustainable Fashion leads with an impressive positive sentiment rate of 87.2%, indicating strong consumer approval and satisfaction with sustainable and ethical manufacturing practices. This high satisfaction rate underscores the growing consumer demand for transparency and highlighting areas for improvement and clearer communication by brands. Table 4 offers a detailed and structured insight into the thematic landscape of green consumption on Instagram, as revealed through Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) analysis. This method of topic modeling has effectively identified key areas of focus and concern among Instagram users discussing green products, neatly categorized by sentiment. The positive sentiments are characterized by five major topics that resonate well with what modern consumers seek in green products. The emphasis on Environmental Impact, Product Quality, Ethical Practices, Innovation, and Community Engagement illustrates a multifaceted appreciation among consumers. This shows a well-rounded interest not just in the environmental benefits of such products but also in their quality, ethical sourcing, innovative features, and the ability to foster community ties. It underscores a holistic approach to sustainability that values both the end product and the process of its creation and distribution. Conversely, the negative sentiments bring to light significant hurdles in the path toward broader acceptance of green products. Product Efficacy, Cost Concerns, and Misleading Claims represent critical feedback areas for producers and marketers of green products. This feedback highlights a gap between consumer expectations and their experiences with green products, pointing to a need for improved product performance, more transparent marketing, and better pricing strategies.

Balcıoğlu et al. - 2025 - Global trends in green consumption analyzing market acceptance and sentiment through Instagram data
paragraphs: This study explores into the complex dynamics of green consumption through Instagram data, providing detailed insights into global consumer perceptions and evaluations of green products. By employing sentiment analysis, word frequency analysis, word cloud visualization, and Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topic modeling, the research offers a comprehensive understanding of consumer attitudes towards green products on social media, reflecting a growing trend of sustainability-focused discourse (Latiff & Safiee, 2015a, b).

The sentiment analysis demonstrated that Instagram users express predominantly positive sentiment toward green products, aligning with global trends in environmental awareness and sustainable consumption (Rustam et al., 2020). However, LDA topic extraction revealed nuanced concerns beyond mere approval or disapproval, such as cost sensitivity and product-specific attributes, which are consistent with the findings of Paul et al. (2016a, b), who noted topics that emerge in discussions about green products, revealing the priorities and concerns of consumers globally.

The LDA model is configured to identify an optimal number of topics by analyzing the coherence and perplexity scores, ensuring meaningful and distinct topic separation. For this analysis, we decide on an optimal number of topics for positive sentiments and a separate set for negative sentiments based on the model's performance and the clarity of the resulting topics. Tables 4 and 5 outlines the extracted topics from Instagram content categorized by sentiment, along with the feature words that characterize each topic.

paragraphs: The results obtained within the scope of the research provide important contributions to the socio-emotional consumer behavior literature regarding green products on broadcast social media platforms such as Instagram. They can also be useful for organizations aiming to connect with consumers. Such insights can help managers develop new strategies by:

• Market Research: Social media data that provides a better understanding of consumer trends can provide insights into focused resource allocation, tailoring products to demand, and even potential marketing processes needed to reduce the risk of market rejection and scalable adoption of sustainability measures (Bhattacharya & Majumdar, 2021). • Product Positioning: Let consumers know how green products can place less strain on the environment and reduce their costs to package up many ideas eco-friendly consumers expect (Zhang et al., 2019). Green Up Your Products Use storytelling and clear-cut labels to turn the crowd into a crowded marketplace. • Influencers and Collaborations: Engage with environmental groups, social media influencers, along with sustainability-focused leaders to generate interest and gain credibility in the green products offered. (Shabbir et al., 2020). Such collaborations help to reach new that financial considerations often shape consumer behavior in sustainable consumption.

Contrary to earlier research that highlighted the dominance of cost and branding over eco-friendliness (Saari, 2016), this study underscores an increasing appreciation for sustainable practices. For example, discussions around eco-conscious footwear and organic coffee reflect consumer emphasis on environmental and ethical attributes (Gonçalves et al., 2016). This shift aligns with recent studies that suggest consumers are increasingly prioritizing sustainability and ethical considerations in purchasing decisions (Nguyen et al., 2018).

Product-Specific Observations 1. Consumer preferences in this category lean heavily towards organic ingredients and health benefits, even over price, supporting findings by Yadav and Pathak (2016) that health and environmental concerns strongly influence green product choices. 2. Consumers prioritize functionality and efficacy, highlighting a pragmatic approach to environmentally responsible consumption, similar to the observations of Dong and Hauschild (2017) regarding consumer adoption of green technologies. 3. Emotional and experiential factors, such as aesthetic appeal and sensory satisfaction, strongly influence preferences in these categories, resonating with studies that emphasize the role of social and emotional value in green consumption (Lin & Huang, 2012a, b).

Negative sentiments focused on issues such as inefficiency and misleading sustainability claims, reflecting barriers noted by Cronin et al. (2011). These concerns provide opportunities for brands to improve transparency and product performance, thereby strengthening consumer trust in sustainable markets.

sections.paper_id: Balcıoğlu et al. - 2025 - Global trends in green consumption analyzing market acceptance and sentiment through Instagram data
paragraphs: This study advances the understanding of global consumer attitudes toward green products by leveraging the analytical power of social media data. Through the innovative use of Instagram data mining-including sentiment analysis, word frequency analysis, word cloud visualization, and LDA topic modeling-this research provides a comprehensive perspective on the factors shaping consumer perceptions and preferences.

The findings highlight that consumer sentiment toward eco-friendly products is generally positive, driven by considerations of price, quality, utility, and environmental impact. These insights underscore the complexities of consumer decision-making processes, revealing significant variations in the prioritization of product attributes across different categories. For instance, practical utility dominates in renewable energy solutions, while emotional and experiential factors influence preferences in sustainable fashion and food.

The study also identifies key barriers to broader acceptance of green products, such as concerns over cost and misleading sustainability claims. Addressing these challenges presents an opportunity for brands to improve product transparency and performance, ultimately enhancing consumer trust and satisfaction in the sustainable marketplace.

By capturing global consumer sentiment and providing actionable insights for businesses and policymakers, this study contributes to the growing body of knowledge on sustainable consumption. It underscores the potential of social media as a tool for understanding and influencing consumer behavior, offering a pathway toward a more sustainable global economy.

sections.paper_id: Balcıoğlu et al. - 2025 - Global trends in green consumption analyzing market acceptance and sentiment through Instagram data
paragraphs: While managerial implications focus on strategic decisions that firms and managers need to make, practical implications provide actionable steps that e-commerce platforms, governments, and businesses can take to encourage the adoption of green products (Rustam et al., 2020). These include:

• Education and Awareness: Public awareness campaigns can be conducted using mass media outlets (television, social networking websites), community gatherings, and the internet to make consumers aware of green products. Its value-adds in terms of environmental conservation and long-term economic sustainability (Zhang et al., 2019). • Green Product Certification: Develop and promote national certification programs for green products. When consumers know just what to expect, they can make informed purchasing decisions (Gouda et al., 2018). • Incentives and Rewards: Introducing a rather serious program that encourages consumers to opt for things eco, in terms of discounts or perhaps earning loyalty points which can be later converted into rewards when they use green products; also, you may reward them in return in the form of some rewards tips by sharing experiences related to eco-friendly products on platforms such as Twitter, Facebook (Hong et al., 2020). • Customer Feedback: Proactively seek and respond to customer feedback to understand their satisfaction and demands for green products. This demonstrates a commitment to continuous improvement and can lead to higher customer satisfaction and loyalty. This can also help to create a positive customer attitude.

This study aims to provide guidance for organizations at different decision-making levels by distinguishing between managerial and practical implications. While practical implications provide concrete actions for immediate implementation, managerial implications provide strategic recommendations for long-term planning.

Because of the diversity of the digital landscape, consumer sentiment may vary between different online ecosystems due to their distinct demographics and user engagement dynamics. Drawing parallels between customer attitudes on sites like Amazon, Twitter, Facebook, and YouTube may reveal how different digital environments influence talks about eco-friendly items. This form of comparison analysis would reveal whether consumer attitudes toward sustainability are universal or unique to certain online environments.

Research that expands its scope globally, particularly into Asia and other countries, can reveal crucial insights into how cultural and regional differences influence eco-friendly consumption. Horizontal comparisons among customers from various locations may reveal distinct driving forces, impediments, and preferences when it comes to purchasing eco-friendly products.

Finally, investigating how the market's acceptance of present green offers influences feelings about upcoming sustainable developments may provide insight into consumer loyalty and the evolution of green purchasing behaviors over time. Observing shifts in consumer perceptions when new green technology enter the market can help us better understand the trajectory of sustainability in consumer domains.

2. The current research focuses on identifying and analyzing individual product attributes that influence consumer sentiment. This approach overlooks the potential interactive effects between different attributes, which could provide deeper insights into how combinations of attributes influence consumer preferences and decisionmaking processes. Future research could benefit from exploring these interactions to understand the synergistic effects of product attributes on consumer sentiment. 3. The reliance on Instagram as the primary data source, while offering a rich dataset of consumer opinions and behaviors, introduces limitations regarding the generalizability of the findings. Social media platforms each have unique user demographics and cultures, which can influence the nature and tone of discussions around green products. Consequently, the insights derived from Instagram data may not fully translate to other platforms or offline consumer behavior. 4. Given the global nature of Instagram, the study captures a wide array of consumer sentiments that span different cultures and regions. However, the platform's user demographics and the language used in the posts analyzed may skew towards certain geographic regions. This raises questions about the applicability of the findings across different cultural and regional contexts, particularly in markets where Instagram is less prevalent or used differently.

sections.paper_id: Balcıoğlu et al. - 2025 - Global trends in green consumption analyzing market acceptance and sentiment through Instagram data
paragraphs: The investigation of social media's influence, particularly Instagram, in creating perceptions of eco-friendly items has considerably increased our understanding of green purchasing trends. This first initiative has identified various areas for further investigation, which are set to expand our understanding of consumers' long-term habits. Future study can not only increase the range of eco-products being studied, but also improve the analytical methodologies used, providing a more nuanced knowledge of the complexities of eco-conscious purchase decisions. Future research has a huge opportunity to broaden its scope by embracing a comprehensive range of eco-conscious products, such as sustainable fashion and electric cars, as well as green cosmetic products and ecologically friendly building materials. A comprehensive examination that spans many industrial landscapes will provide light on the whole range of green consumer practices, as well as the specific preferences and challenges that each sector faces.

A different intriguing avenue of research is looking into how multiple product characteristics cross statistically. Investigating how factors such as cost concerns interact with environmental benefits to affect purchasing decisions can give researchers with in-depth knowledge about the trade-offs customers are willing to make to achieve sustainability.
